title: The staircase they want to remove
summary: On a steep hill in Valparaíso, a city planner and an elderly resident disagree about a crumbling public staircase the city wants to replace with a road — one thinks in budgets and safety, the other in the life the steps hold.

body.speaker: Engineer Rojas
body.text: Señora Beltrán, thank you for coming. The Escalera Echaurren is two hundred steps of broken concrete; three have fallen this year, one seriously. Even repaired, it stays useless to an ambulance or anyone in a wheelchair. The plan is to replace it with a narrow switchback road.

body.speaker: Señora Beltrán
body.text: I have climbed those steps every day for sixty-one years. I will not pretend the concrete is not broken; my knee tells me so each morning. But before you replace them, see what you would be paving over.

body.speaker: Engineer Rojas
body.text: I am listening. But understand my side. I have a duty to the people who fell, and to the old man on step one hundred and ten who could not get a stretcher to his door during his heart attack. A road, for me, is a matter of life.

body.speaker: Señora Beltrán
body.text: I know about Don Emilio's heart attack; I held his hand on those steps while we waited. So do not think I am only sentimental. On those steps there are nineteen front doors, and every neighbour passes every other on the way up. We rest on the same three landings and notice when a curtain has not opened. The steps make us share the climb.

body.speaker: Engineer Rojas
body.text: That is a real thing, and I will not dismiss it. But I cannot put 'we notice when a curtain has not opened' into a safety report. I can only put the man who waited too long for a stretcher. A death I can measure; what you describe I cannot.

body.speaker: Señora Beltrán
body.text: Then let me give you something you can measure. The mural on the seventh landing, painted by schoolchildren in 1974, is in every guidebook, and the cafés below live on the tourists who climb for it. Your road removes the mural and the reason anyone comes; three streets will empty. Put that next to the stretcher in your report.

body.speaker: Engineer Rojas
body.text: You have made my job harder, and I think you meant to. You are right that I was weighing a death against 'sentiment', and that was not honest, because tourists and cafés are money — the language my report speaks. The real choice is uglier: a man's access to an ambulance against a neighbourhood's way of life.

body.speaker: Señora Beltrán
body.text: Perhaps you do not have to choose so completely. The hill is wide. Could the road climb the far side, where there are fewer doors and no mural, and reach Don Emilio's house from above? Then the ambulance has its route and the steps keep their landings. It costs more, but you would not pave over the only thing that makes this a place, not an address.

body.speaker: Engineer Rojas
body.text: A road on the far side. It is dearer, the slope is worse, and I cannot promise the budget. But it is the first idea today that does not ask me to choose between a life and a community, and for that I will fight. I came to tell you a decision; I leave with a better one. Walk me down, slowly. I want to see the landings.

annotations.meaningVi: một con đường ngoằn ngoèo hẹp (gập lên dốc)
chiếu nghỉ (mặt bằng giữa các bậc thang)
gạt bỏ nó / coi nhẹ nó
trong mọi sách hướng dẫn du lịch
khả năng tiếp cận xe cứu thương
không thể hứa về ngân sách
một địa chỉ (chỉ là nơi chốn vô hồn)

grammarNotes.title: Second conditional + present perfect for lived experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng câu điều kiện loại 2 cho tình huống giả định: 'I want you to understand what you would be paving over', 'you would not be paving over the only thing that makes this a place', 'Could the road climb the far side...?' (could để đề xuất lịch sự, giả định). Bài cũng dùng hiện tại hoàn thành cho trải nghiệm sống dài: 'I have climbed those steps every day for sixty-one years', 'Three people have fallen this year', 'You have just made my job harder'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses the second conditional for hypothetical situations: 'I want you to understand what you would be paving over', 'you would not be paving over the only thing that makes this a place', and 'Could the road climb the far side...?' (could for a polite, hypothetical proposal). It also uses the present perfect for long lived experience: 'I have climbed those steps every day for sixty-one years', 'Three people have fallen this year', 'You have just made my job harder'.

translationVi:
Engineer Rojas: Bà Beltrán, cảm ơn bà đã đến. Cầu thang Escalera Echaurren là hai trăm bậc bê tông vỡ nát; năm nay ba người đã ngã, một người nặng. Dù có sửa, nó vẫn vô dụng với xe cứu thương hay bất cứ ai ngồi xe lăn. Kế hoạch là thay nó bằng một con đường ngoằn ngoèo hẹp.

Señora Beltrán: Tôi đã leo những bậc đó mỗi ngày suốt sáu mươi mốt năm. Tôi sẽ không vờ rằng bê tông không vỡ; cái đầu gối của tôi nói thế mỗi sáng. Nhưng trước khi anh thay chúng, hãy xem anh sẽ lát đè lên cái gì.

Engineer Rojas: Tôi đang nghe. Nhưng hãy hiểu phía tôi. Tôi có nghĩa vụ với những người đã ngã, và với ông cụ ở bậc một trăm mười không thể đưa cáng đến cửa nhà trong cơn đau tim. Với tôi, một con đường là chuyện sống còn.

Señora Beltrán: Tôi biết về cơn đau tim của ông Emilio; chính tôi nắm tay ông trên những bậc thang đó trong lúc chờ. Vậy đừng nghĩ tôi chỉ đang ướt át. Trên những bậc đó có mười chín cánh cửa trước, và mỗi người hàng xóm đều đi qua mọi cánh cửa khác trên đường lên. Chúng tôi nghỉ chân trên cùng ba chiếu nghỉ và để ý khi một tấm rèm chưa mở. Những bậc thang buộc chúng tôi cùng chia sẻ cuộc leo.

Engineer Rojas: Đó là điều có thật, và tôi sẽ không gạt bỏ. Nhưng tôi không thể đưa 'chúng tôi để ý khi một tấm rèm chưa mở' vào báo cáo an toàn. Tôi chỉ có thể đưa người đàn ông đã chờ cáng quá lâu. Một cái chết tôi đo được; cái bà mô tả thì tôi không.

Señora Beltrán: Vậy để tôi cho anh thứ anh đo được. Bức tranh tường ở chiếu nghỉ thứ bảy, do học sinh vẽ năm 1974, có trong mọi sách hướng dẫn, và các quán cà phê dưới chân sống nhờ những du khách leo lên vì nó. Con đường của anh xóa bức tranh và lý do người ta đến; ba con phố sẽ trống. Hãy đặt điều đó cạnh cái cáng trong báo cáo của anh.

Engineer Rojas: Bà vừa làm việc của tôi khó hơn, và tôi nghĩ bà cố ý. Bà đúng khi nói tôi đã đem một cái chết ra cân với 'tình cảm', và điều đó không trung thực, vì du khách và quán cà phê là tiền — thứ ngôn ngữ báo cáo của tôi nói. Lựa chọn thật xấu xí hơn: khả năng tiếp cận xe cứu thương của một người cân với cả một lối sống của khu phố.

Señora Beltrán: Có lẽ anh không phải chọn hẳn một bên. Ngọn đồi rộng. Liệu con đường có thể leo phía bên kia, nơi ít cửa hơn và không có tranh tường, rồi đến nhà ông Emilio từ phía trên không? Khi đó xe cứu thương có lối, và những bậc thang giữ được chiếu nghỉ. Tốn kém hơn, nhưng anh sẽ không lát đè lên thứ duy nhất khiến đây là một nơi chốn, không chỉ một địa chỉ.

Engineer Rojas: Một con đường ở phía bên kia. Nó đắt hơn, độ dốc tệ hơn, và tôi không thể hứa về ngân sách. Nhưng đó là ý đầu tiên hôm nay không bắt tôi chọn giữa một mạng người và một cộng đồng, và vì thế tôi sẽ đấu tranh cho nó. Tôi đến để báo một quyết định; tôi ra về với một quyết định hay hơn. Đưa tôi đi xuống, chậm thôi. Tôi muốn nhìn những chiếu nghỉ.

questions.prompt: What is the city's plan for the Escalera Echaurren?
options: To replace the two hundred broken steps with a narrow switchback road
questions.explanation: 'The plan is to replace it with a narrow switchback road.'

questions.prompt: What safety reasons does Rojas give?
options: Three people have fallen this year, and the steps are useless to an ambulance, delivery van, or wheelchair
The steps are too steep to climb at all
questions.explanation: 'Three people have fallen this year, one seriously... useless to an ambulance, to a delivery van, to anyone in a wheelchair.'

questions.prompt: How does Señora Beltrán establish she is not just sentimental?
options: She admits the concrete is broken and reveals she held Don Emilio's hand on the steps during his heart attack
She says she never uses the steps
She agrees the road is best
She offers to pay for the road
questions.explanation: 'I will not pretend the concrete is not broken... I was the one who held his hand on those steps while we waited.'

questions.prompt: What social life do the steps hold, according to Beltrán?
options: Nineteen front doors; neighbours pass every door, greet, rest on shared landings, and notice when a curtain has not opened
questions.explanation: 'there are nineteen front doors... We greet, we rest on the same three landings, we notice when a curtain has not opened.'

questions.prompt: Why does Rojas say his 'hands are not as free as you think'?
options: He can put a death in a safety report but cannot put unmeasurable things like noticing a curtain
He has been ordered by his boss
He owns one of the houses
questions.explanation: 'I cannot put "we notice when a curtain has not opened" into a safety report. I can only put the man who waited too long for a stretcher.'

questions.prompt: What measurable argument does Beltrán then give him?
options: The 1974 mural draws tourists in every guidebook, the cafés live on them, and the road would empty three streets
The steps are cheaper to repair than build a road
The road would flood in winter
The hill is protected by law
questions.explanation: 'The mural on the seventh landing... is in every guidebook... The cafés at the bottom live on those tourists.'

questions.prompt: How does Rojas admit he had been unfair?
options: He had weighed a death against mere 'sentiment', but the tourists and cafés are money — exactly the language his report speaks
He admits he never read the safety data
He confesses he wants the steps gone for himself
He says he made up the heart attack
questions.explanation: 'that was not honest of me, because the tourists and the cafés are money, and money is exactly the language my report speaks.'

questions.prompt: How does Rojas restate the real choice?
options: A man's access to an ambulance against a neighbourhood's whole way of living — and he cannot have both on the same hill
questions.explanation: 'It is a man's access to an ambulance against a neighbourhood's whole way of living, and I cannot have both on the same hill.'

options: Run the road up the far side, where there are fewer doors and no mural, reaching Don Emilio's house from above
Ban all cars from the hill
Demolish the mural and keep the steps
Move Don Emilio to a new house
questions.explanation: 'Could the road climb the far side... and reach Don Emilio's house from above? Then the ambulance has its route, and the steps keep their landings.'

questions.prompt: How does Rojas respond to the far-side road idea?
options: It is costlier and the slope is worse and he cannot promise the budget, but it does not force a choice between a life and a community, so he will fight for it
He decides to keep the original road plan
questions.explanation: 'it is the first idea today that does not ask me to choose between a life and a community, and for that reason I will fight for it.'

questions.prompt: In context, what does Beltrán mean by 'a place and not just an address'?
options: Somewhere with shared human life, not merely a location on a map
questions.explanation: She contrasts the living community of the steps with a bare 'address'.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A planning conflict between measurable safety and unmeasurable community life is resolved not by one side winning but by both being honest, which opens a costlier third option that serves both.
Old staircases should always be preserved.
questions.explanation: Both speakers concede points; the honest reframing produces the far-side road compromise.

On those steps there are nineteen front doors, and every neighbour passes every other on the way up. We rest on the same three {{b6}} and notice when a curtain has not opened. The steps make us share the climb.
Engineer Rojas: That is a real thing, and I will not {{b7}} it. But I cannot put 'we notice when a curtain has not opened' into a safety report. I can only put the man who waited too long for a {{b4}}. A death I can measure; what you describe I cannot.
Señora Beltrán: Then let me give you something you can measure. The real choice is uglier: a man's access to an ambulance against a neighbourhood's way of life.
Señora Beltrán: Perhaps you do not have to choose so completely. The hill is wide. Could the road climb the far side, where there are fewer doors and no mural, and reach Don Emilio's house from above? Then the ambulance has its route and the steps keep their {{b10}}. It costs more, but you would not pave over the only thing that makes this a place, not an address.
Engineer Rojas: A road on the far side. It is dearer, the slope is worse, and I cannot promise the budget. But it is the first idea today that does not ask me to choose between a life and a {{b11}}, and for that I will fight. I came to tell you a decision; I leave with a better one. Walk me down, slowly. I want to see the landings.

blanks.explanation: Two hundred steps of broken concrete.
What you would be paving over.
Could not get a stretcher to his door.
Do not think I am only being sentimental.
We rest on the same three landings.
The mural on the seventh landing.
The tourists and the cafés are money.
Choose between a life and a community.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Beltrán wins ground only when she translates community life into something the engineer can measure — tourists and money. Think of something valuable in your own neighbourhood that 'cannot go in a report'. How would you make a decision-maker see its worth without pretending it is something it isn't?
